questions.question: Why does this course describe being understood as a fundamental human right rather than a luxury?
options: Because when someone cannot make their needs known, their dignity, wellbeing and safety are all put at risk
Because it is required only for people who can already speak clearly
Because it saves the care service time and money
Because the law says everyone must speak English

questions.question: According to the course, communication supports choice because people can only make real decisions when:
options: A relative decides on their behalf
Information reaches them in a way they understand
They agree with the care worker
They are able to write things down

questions.question: What does this course explicitly state it does NOT replace?
options: The person's own family
Reading the care plan
Supervised, hands-on training and the assessment and advice of a qualified speech and language therapist
The care worker's own judgement entirely

questions.question: After a stroke, a person may have aphasia. How does the course describe this?
options: A loss of intelligence
A permanent inability to hear
A form of sensory loss affecting sight
Difficulty using or understanding language, even though their intelligence is unchanged

questions.question: An autistic person may communicate differently. Which point does the course make?
options: They may take language literally and find busy environments overwhelming
They cannot understand any language
They always prefer to communicate in writing
They do not have additional communication needs

questions.question: Which conditions does the course name as physical conditions affecting the muscles used for speech?
options: Dementia and stroke
Cerebral palsy and motor neurone disease
Hearing loss and sight loss
Learning disability and autism

questions.question: What warning does the course repeat about someone who finds speech difficult?
options: Always speak to their relative instead
Assume they cannot understand anything
Never assume that difficulty speaking means difficulty thinking
They should not be offered choices

questions.question: What is the care worker's role in supporting communication, according to the course?
options: To assess needs and prescribe tools
To carry out speech and language therapy
To change the specialist plan whenever they think best
To support communication and follow the plan that has been put in place

questions.question: What does a speech and language therapist (SALT) do?
options: Assesses a person's communication, and often eating and swallowing, and advises on how best to support them
Only provides hearing aids
Writes the entire care plan alone with no input from anyone
Replaces the care worker's daily role

questions.question: If a specialist communication approach is not working, what should a care worker do?
options: Quietly change the plan on their own
Report it so it can be reviewed
Stop supporting the person
Ignore it and carry on regardless

questions.question: How does the course define a communication barrier?
options: A rule set by the speech and language therapist
A type of hearing aid
Anything that gets in the way of a message being sent, received, or understood
A section of the care plan

questions.question: The course says to 'presume competence'. What does this mean?
options: Assume the person cannot understand until proven otherwise
Assume a relative should speak for them
Assume competence tests are needed first
Assume the person has something to say and can understand, and adjust your approach until you find what works

questions.question: Someone wears hearing aids but the television is loud and the room is dim. What is the best approach?
options: Turn the television down, make sure the room is well lit and the hearing aids are working, then face the person and speak clearly
Speak much louder over the television
Carry on regardless, as changing the environment is not your role
Assume they will not understand and speak to their relative instead

questions.question: What does the total communication approach mean?
options: Using speech alone but very clearly
Using every available method together to help a person understand and be understood
Only using signing
Communicating solely through written notes

questions.question: What is an object of reference?
options: A picture symbol on a communication board
A written label attached to a door
A real object used to stand for an activity, person, or place
A signed word in Makaton

questions.question: A person you support takes a long time to respond to questions. What is the most person-centred approach?
options: Answer for them to save time
Ask several questions quickly so they only respond once
Assume no immediate response means they have nothing to say
Ask one question at a time, then pause and give them plenty of time to respond in their own way

questions.question: How does the course describe easy read information?
options: Simple words and clear pictures side by side, one idea at a time
Long, detailed text with no pictures
Information written only in symbols
A spoken recording of a document

questions.question: How is Makaton mainly used?
options: It is used silently, without any speaking
Signs and symbols are used at the same time as speech to support understanding
It replaces speech completely and is used only within the Deaf community
It is a written code only trained therapists can understand

questions.question: How does the course distinguish British Sign Language from Makaton?
options: They are exactly the same thing
BSL uses only symbols and no signs
BSL is a full language in its own right used mainly within the Deaf community; Makaton is used together with the spoken word
BSL is used together with the spoken word; Makaton replaces speech

questions.question: What is a communication passport?
options: A legal identity document
A travel permit for people with communication needs
A type of high-tech AAC device
A personal document, written with the person, that describes how someone communicates

questions.question: What does AAC stand for and cover?
options: Augmentative and alternative communication, methods that add to or replace speech
Assisted Access Care, a type of care plan
Automated Audio Communication, a phone system
Advanced Assessment of Communication, a SALT test

questions.question: How should an AAC device be treated?
options: As an optional extra that can be put away when staff are busy
As the person's voice: kept charged, clean, and always within reach
As equipment only the SALT may touch
As a device to speak on the person's behalf while they wait

questions.question: When is a qualified interpreter particularly needed?
options: For every routine daily greeting
Only when no family member is available
For important conversations such as health appointments or decisions about care
Only when the person uses Makaton

questions.question: What does the Accessible Information Standard require of NHS and adult social care services?
options: To provide every document in easy read to absolutely everyone
To translate every document into every language automatically
To apply only within hospitals and not social care
To identify, record, meet, and share people's information and communication needs so they can access care equally

questions.question: A person nods and says yes after you explain an activity. How should you best check they have understood?
options: Gently ask them to show you, or tell you in their own way, what will happen next
Accept the nod as proof of full understanding
Repeat exactly the same words, only louder
Move on quickly before they change their mind

questions.question: Communication is described in the course as a fundamental human right linked to dignity, choice and safety.
options: True
False

questions.question: As a care worker, it is your job to assess a person's communication needs and decide which specialist tools they should use.
options: True
False

questions.question: If a person cannot speak clearly, it means they cannot understand or think clearly.
options: True
False

questions.question: Many communication barriers are created by the environment or by how we communicate, which means we can often remove them.
options: True
False

questions.question: It is fine to move a person's AAC device out of the way when you are busy, as long as you give it back later.
options: True
False

questions.question: With Makaton, you usually sign only the main key words in a sentence while speaking.
options: True
False

questions.question: Easy read is a suitable format for everyone and always replaces the need to talk with someone.
options: True
False

questions.question: An object of reference should always mean the same thing each time it is used.
options: True
False

questions.question: According to the course, why is communication so important for the people you support? (Select all that apply)
options: It supports dignity, as being heard is central to being treated as a person
It supports choice, as information reaches people in a way they understand
It supports safety, as a person can report pain, worry, or a mistake
It is only important for people who can speak fluently

questions.question: Which of the following are named in the course as sources of additional communication needs? (Select all that apply)
options: Learning disability
Dementia
Stroke causing aphasia
Enjoying reading
Being a confident public speaker

questions.question: Which actions help reduce barriers to communication? (Select all that apply)
options: Reduce background noise
Make sure the room is well lit so your face can be seen
Check that any aids the person uses are working and to hand
Rush to fill every silence

questions.question: The total communication approach may combine which methods? (Select all that apply)
options: Speech and gesture
Pictures and objects
Signing and writing
Relying on one fixed method for every person
Refusing to use any communication aids

questions.question: What might a communication passport include? (Select all that apply)
options: How I communicate, including the words, signs, sounds, and gestures I use
How to support me to understand and be understood
What my responses mean, such as how I show yes, no, pain, or distress
My bank account details

questions.question: Which are features of good record-keeping about communication? (Select all that apply)
options: Be specific: describe what you did and how the person responded
Keep it current: update records as the person's needs change
Share it: make sure new information reaches the care plan and the team
Keep the knowledge only in your own head

questions.question: Which steps does the Accessible Information Standard set out for people's information and communication needs? (Select all that apply)
options: Identify the person's needs
Record those needs clearly in their records
Meet the needs and share them, with consent, so others can too
Ignore needs that seem inconvenient

questions.question: Loss of the ability to use or understand language after a stroke is called ___.
A qualified professional who assesses a person's communication and advises on how best to support them is a speech and language ___.
A real object used to stand for an activity, person, or place is called an object of ___.
A personal document, written with the person, that describes how someone communicates is called a communication ___.
The language programme that uses signs and symbols alongside speech, often used by people with a learning disability, is called ___.
